title: T-286 spawn-zero assertion is tautological under setVerifyOverride
description: T-286 claims to verify that withVerifyStub keeps verifySpot from spawning a subprocess. The harness's withVerifyStub installs a module-level override (setVerifyOverride) that short-circuits runVerification's first branch BEFORE the compile adapter's spawn site (verify.ts:65-67). With the override active, the compile branch (verify.ts:69-101) is unreachable, so 'spawn was not called' is trivially satisfied — any override fn returning early would pass. The test does not actually exercise verify.ts's compile path; it exercises only the override seam. The genuine 'compile path does not spawn when stubbed via opts.spawn' invariant is not tested because the harness uses a different (global) seam.
impact: If a future refactor moves the compile spawn site BEFORE the override check, T-286 still passes silently. The test was promoted to a regression after the cycle-4 amendment but does not regress what its name claims.
recommendation: Either (a) drop the override seam from verify.ts entirely and have the harness wire verifySpot through opts.spawn (mirroring the M005 carry-forward), so T-286's assertion exercises the real adapter; or (b) split T-286 into two cases: one with override active (current), one with NO override + a no-op opts.spawn that records but never calls real spawn — the latter would catch spawn-leakage past the seam.

title: verify.ts reintroduces the global mutable seam M005 carry-forward removed from preflight
description: Cycle 4 explicitly retired preflight.ts's setSpawnOverride + module-level Map (carry-forward M005 / R2-003) in favor of per-call ProbeOptions.spawn injection. The new verify.ts ships a new module-level `let _verifyOverride: VerifyOverrideFn | undefined` plus an exported `setVerifyOverride()` that mutates it — exactly the pattern M005 condemned. The contract specified that 'Spawn injection seam parallels the probe pattern (opts.spawn?: SpawnFn) so tests stub the build without invoking the real pnpm.' The implementation honors that with `opts?.spawn` AND adds a parallel global seam the contract did not authorize.
impact: Cycle 5+ tests can mutate verification globally between tests, creating cross-test pollution and parallel-run hazards — the same class of bug M005 was filed to eliminate. Reviewer dimension `simplicity` may also flag the duplication.
recommendation: Drop _verifyOverride / setVerifyOverride. Have the harness's withVerifyStub install a SpawnFn that returns predictable stdout/stderr (or wrap verifySpot via a per-call opts.spawn pass-through), matching the cycle-4 contract's spawn-injection-only seam.

title: T-224 SHA-256 baseline only runs on the author's machine; CI silently skips byte-equality
description: T-224 uses `it.skipIf(!fs.existsSync(REFERENCE_SOURCE))` where REFERENCE_SOURCE is `~/workspace/deepbook-sandbox-evaluation-apps/01-orderbook-viewer/src/App.tsx`. On any machine without that checkout (CI, fresh dev clones, the reviewer's box), T-224 silently skips. The fallback T-225 only asserts existence + size > 0, which a one-byte file passes. The 'byte-for-byte snapshot' invariant from contract A9 is therefore unverifiable in CI — only the author can ever fail this test. The pre-computed REFERENCE_APP_TSX_SHA256_AT_AUTHOR constant exists but is never compared against the bundled file when the source is absent.
impact: Silent drift between paths/01-orderbook-viewer/reference/App.tsx and the upstream source-of-truth ships unnoticed. The test is a no-op in CI.
recommendation: Compare the bundled file's SHA-256 against REFERENCE_APP_TSX_SHA256_AT_AUTHOR unconditionally (the constant IS the cross-machine ground truth — the contract calls it 'the author's recorded hash'). Use the source-existence skip ONLY for the second 'sanity check the source on this machine' branch, not the bundled-vs-recorded check.

title: T-254 RegistryWarning union check is string-grep; passes if kind literals appear in comments
description: T-254 enforces 'RegistryWarning is a discriminated union of exactly eight kinds' via `content.indexOf("'" + k + "'")` for each expected kind plus a negative regex on `kind: string`. The positive test passes if each kind literal appears ANYWHERE in warnings.ts source (e.g., in a docblock comment listing the kinds). It does not verify that the kinds are members of an actual TypeScript discriminated union. The negative `looseRe` test only catches the most literal form `kind: string` and would not catch `kind: K` where K is an unconstrained type alias.
impact: A stub warnings.ts with the kinds buried in comments and a permissive shape would pass T-254. The companion T-256 runtime check (`isRegistryKind` runtime list) is also not a real type-narrowing assertion; the comment correctly notes 'the load-bearing assertion is the tsc --noEmit check (T-282)'.
recommendation: Augment with a small TypeScript snippet compiled via the project tsc setup (or asserted at type-level via a `Equal<>` helper) that fails compile if RegistryWarning lacks discriminated narrowing. Alternatively, parse the AST and assert RegistryWarning is a TypeAliasDeclaration whose type is a UnionType of literal kinds.

title: T-291 only asserts placeholder presence in hint.md; never exercises substitution against rung payload
description: Contract scope says: 'Personalization substitution is scoped exclusively to spot prompts (and the rung markdown payloads — hint/reference/auto-write — when those are loaded; cycle 5 wires the rungs but cycle 4 establishes the substitution rule)'. T-291's name claims 'hint exercises substitution' but the body only `fs.readFileSync(...hint.md)` and asserts the file content matches `/\{\{\s*pool_subset\s*\}\}/`. No call to substitutePromptOnly is made against the loaded markdown; nothing verifies that the substitution rule (when wired) would resolve correctly against the rung payload. The test passes for any hint.md containing the placeholder substring even if the substitution function would reject it.
impact: Since cycle 5 will wire the rungs, this is non-blocking now. But the test name overstates coverage — a future review of 'is the rung-substitution rule covered' may incorrectly conclude yes.
recommendation: Either rename to 'hint.md contains a {{ pool_subset }} placeholder for cycle-5 wiring' (clarifying it is presence-only), or add a call: `expect(substitutePromptOnly(hintBytes, { pool_subset: 'DEEP_SUI', poll_interval_ms: 3000 }).includes('DEEP_SUI')).toBe(true)`.

title: Contract called for substitutePromptOnly internal-assertion guard test; tests only assert via static greps
description: Contract `tests/personalization.test.ts` line says: 'Negative test: passing target_file/target_range strings through substitutePromptOnly is forbidden by an internal assertion (test that the function throws on a known guard sentinel) — the test enforces the AC-6.3 boundary at the unit level.' The shipped tests T-214 and T-215 are static greps over engine source: T-214 checks `{{...}}` placeholders don't appear in non-personalization engine files; T-215 checks no caller passes the four field names through substitutePromptOnly via a 160-char window heuristic around each call site. Neither exercises a runtime guard inside substitutePromptOnly. There is no test asserting `expect(() => substitutePromptOnly('{{ x }}', { x: 'val' }, { sentinel: 'target_file' })).toThrow()` or similar.
impact: If substitutePromptOnly is later refactored to accept a path/range parameter, only the static greps catch it — and only if a caller's surrounding 160 chars happen to contain one of the four field names. A caller that destructures the field into a local variable named `prompt` first would defeat T-215 and ship a violation.
recommendation: Add a runtime sentinel/guard test as the contract specified. E.g., have substitutePromptOnly take an optional `field?: string` arg used only by tests; pass `field='target_file'` and assert it throws. Or more simply, a positive assertion that the function only ever returns a string without mutating any property named target_file/target_range/verification.
evidence:
describe('substitutePromptOnly — substitution scope guards (AC-6.3)', () => {
  it('T-214: ... engine source contains zero {{...}} placeholders outside personalization.ts ...

title: T-267 round-trip cannot distinguish whether saveState persists auto_write_attempted
description: T-267 seeds a state literal with `auto_write_attempted: false` explicitly, calls saveState, calls loadState, and expects deep-equal. The amended cycle-4 loader normalizes absent rung fields to `auto_write_attempted: false` (mcp/server/src/schemas/state.ts:94). Therefore: (a) if saveState writes the field verbatim AND loadState reads it, deep-equal passes; (b) if saveState OMITS the field at write time AND loadState defaults absent → false, deep-equal STILL passes. T-267 alone cannot tell these apart. The synthesis-notes flagged this as a fixture amendment; the orchestrator's amendment was correct but the resulting test is no longer load-bearing for whether the persistence layer carries the new field.
impact: T-267 is no longer the round-trip canary; T-269 (true) and T-271 (default-false-on-absent-write) together cover the actual round-trip semantics, so coverage is intact in aggregate. But anyone reading T-267 alone may misread it as a strong round-trip assertion when it is no longer one.
recommendation: Either rename T-267 to clarify it is a 'durability path remains intact (no exception on write/read)' regression, or seed the test with a rung value where one of the four fields is `true` so deep-equal would catch a saveState that drops fields. The latter is one keystroke and restores the field-level round-trip claim.
evidence: 'p1-spot-1': { hint_used: false, reference_shown: false, auto_completed: false, auto_write_attempted: false }

title: T-231 'state persists via saveState round-trip' uses fully-mocked state.ts; not an integration test
description: T-231's name and body comment claim 'integration with state.ts loadState/saveState contract'. But the test file mocks `'../mcp/server/src/state.js'` at the top with `vi.mock(...)` and `stateMock.{loadState,saveState}` are vi.fn() stubs. The 'round-trip' is purely in-memory: saveState writes into a closure variable `saved`, loadState returns `saved`. No actual file I/O, JSON serialization, schema validation, or atomic-rename path is exercised. A bug where selectPath produces a state that the real state.ts would reject (e.g., bad schema) is not caught.
impact: Coverage gap: selectPath's interface to state.ts is tested only at the call-shape level. The cycle-4 harness.lesson.test.ts T-283 exercises the real file path through MCP, so this is not a hard gap — but the test name oversells.
recommendation: Either drop the 'integration with state.ts loadState/saveState contract' phrasing from the description (it's a unit test of selectPath's call shape), or add a parallel non-mocked variant that lets the real saveState write to a tempdir.
evidence: it('T-231: state persists via saveState round-trip (integration with state.ts loadState/saveState contract)', async () => {

title: T-258 phase-engine warning kinds tested by string-grep only — no shape or emission test
description: T-258 asserts the three new warning kinds (`phase-engine-phases-load-failed`, `personalization-validation-failed`, `verification-mode-unsupported`) appear in warnings.ts via `content.indexOf(...) > -1`. No test verifies that any code path actually emits these warnings, that they have the expected payload shape (e.g., `phase-engine-phases-load-failed` carrying `slug` and `reason` per contract), or that the discriminated union narrows them correctly. The contract specified payloads (`carries slug and reason`, `carries errors: string[]`, `carries mode: string`); T-258 does not assert those payload fields exist.
impact: A warnings.ts that mentions the kinds in comments would pass T-258. Downstream consumers relying on the structured payload would discover the schema mismatch only at runtime.
recommendation: Add field-level assertions: import the union type at runtime via a discriminator switch, or write a small test that constructs each warning and TypeScript-checks the field set. Equivalently, parse the warnings.ts AST and assert each new interface declares the specified fields.
